region_of_origin: China (Guangdong / Cantonese tradition, with Zhejiang matcha and Guangxi Lipu taro)
food_subgroup: Steamed milk custard / Chinese milk pudding

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chinese double-layer steamed milk custard (shuang pi nai). The closest defensible parent is the generic custard food product class FOODON_03301116.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for shuang pi nai or matcha-taro steamed milk custard. A00LC (milk-based desserts) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Chinese steamed milk custard preparation. SNOMED CT does not cover this food at a useful level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one individual steamed cup of double-layer milk custard as typically served in a Chinese dessert shop.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Whole milk (FDC ID 1097512), Egg white raw (FDC ID 172183), Taro cooked (FDC ID 170071), Matcha green tea powder (FDC ID 2003571), and Sugar (FDC ID 1983); weighted recipe estimation for shuang pi nai base with matcha and taro additions.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for matcha-taro double-layer milk custard exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted recipe model using the following approximate proportions per 100 g of finished product: whole milk 55%, egg white 15%, taro puree 20%, matcha paste 5%, sugar 5%. Values are cross-referenced against published shuang pi nai recipe analyses and USDA component data. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of taro (Colocasia esculenta) and milk-based desserts — analogous estimate based on: (1) Taro GI ~53 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); (2) Whole milk GI ~27 (Foster-Powell K et al., 2002, Am J Clin Nutr); (3) Matcha green tea — negligible glycemic contribution; blended weighted GI estimated at ~48 for the composite custard.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283; Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC (2002) Am J Clin Nutr 76(1):5-56
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for matcha-taro double-layer milk custard. GI estimated at 48 using a weighted blend of taro GI (53, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) and whole milk GI (27, Foster-Powell et al. 2002), weighted by carbohydrate contribution from each component. Matcha and egg white contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (48 × 15.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 14.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~52 mg per 100 g) with beneficial potassium (~185 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 104 mg sodium per standard 200 g serving, this dessert is low in sodium and unlikely to adversely affect blood pressure; however, individuals with hypertension should remain mindful of total daily sodium intake from all sources and limit added sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~48) and meaningful sugar content (~9.8 g per 100 g) from added sugar and taro
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 48 (low category) and approximately 19.6 g of sugar per 200 g serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dessert in moderation, monitor portion size carefully, and account for the glycemic load of approximately 14.9 g per serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.3 g per 100 g) from whole milk, partially offset by low trans fat and beneficial micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 200 g serving provides approximately 4.6 g of saturated fat, which is within acceptable limits for most cardiovascular disease management plans; individuals with established cardiovascular disease should limit overall saturated fat intake and consider frequency of consumption as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Matcha and Taro Double-Layer Milk Custard (抹茶芋头双皮奶, Shuang Pi Nai) is a contemporary Cantonese-style steamed milk dessert that layers two distinct flavoured custards in a single cup. The base layer is a classic shuang pi nai — a silky, protein-set steamed milk custard made from fresh whole milk and egg whites — while the upper layer incorporates either a vibrant Zhejiang dry-matcha paste or a dense handcrafted taro puree sourced from Guangxi Lipu taro, one of China's most prized taro varieties known for its purple-flecked flesh and intensely starchy, fragrant character. The dessert is steamed daily in limited quantities, resulting in a delicate, wobbly texture with a thin milk skin on the surface — the hallmark of authentic shuang pi nai. The matcha layer delivers a grassy, slightly bitter umami note, while the taro layer contributes a naturally sweet, earthy, and floral flavour. Together, the two layers create a visually striking and texturally complex dessert. Nutritionally, each 200 g serving provides approximately 224 kcal, 8.4 g protein, 7.6 g fat, and 31 g carbohydrate, with meaningful calcium (~236 mg) from the milk base. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 48 places it in the low GI category, making it a relatively moderate-impact dessert compared to many confections. Clinically, the dessert is low in sodium and suitable for most dietary patterns in moderation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size due to the sugar content from taro and added sugar. The dish is served at Chinese dessert shops (糖水铺) in Singapore and is representative of the modern fusion of traditional Cantonese milk custard craft with premium regional Chinese ingredients.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for matcha-taro double-layer milk custard exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted recipe model using USDA FoodData Central component data for whole milk, egg white, taro, matcha powder, and sugar. GI is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database values for taro and whole milk. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are recipe-estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods; no direct approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or an HPB-equivalent entry if one becomes available. 2. The food_group has been corrected from 'Beverages' (as listed in the field survey source) to 'Desserts', which accurately reflects the nature of shuang pi nai as a steamed milk custard dessert. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (weighted blend of taro GI 53 and milk GI 27); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as whole milk and egg white contain negligible industrially produced trans fats at the quantities used, and no hydrogenated fats are present in this recipe. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.0) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.2) are estimated from USDA component data for whole milk and egg white. 6. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported or calculable with sufficient confidence from the component estimation method used; these are not confirmed zero values. Specifically: no -1 values remain in this record as all fields have been populated with estimates or confirmed values from USDA FoodData Central component data. 7. Halal status is assumed based on ingredients (milk, egg white, taro, matcha, sugar) with no pork or alcohol; formal halal certification of the restaurant has not been verified. 8. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate based on typical Chinese dessert shop portion sizes for shuang pi nai; actual restaurant portion may vary.
